items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Cohen's d of 0.5 represents what size of effect?
items.why: Correct answer: Medium. “Small” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Open Science Collaboration 2015 found what proportion of psychology studies replicated?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 36%. “12%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Open Science Collaboration 2015 replication rate was approximately what percent?
items.why: The real figure is 36. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
